input.content:
schemaVersion: 1
session: dotprod
lanes:
  llm:
    - prompt: >-
        Tonight's aha was "the dot product is a similarity meter." Ask an
        LLM to walk you through scaled dot-product attention from "Attention
        Is All You Need" using that exact meter language: which two vectors
        get pressed against each other, what the raw meter reading looks
        like before softmax, and why dividing by sqrt(d_k) is needed once
        the dimension gets large. Push it to connect the scaling term back
        to "what the meter gets wrong."
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer names the query and key vectors explicitly, shows the
        reading growing with dimension count even when direction hasn't
        changed, and explains sqrt(d_k) as damage control on that drift, not
        a magic constant.
    - prompt: >-
        The breadcrumb question was "what is the cheapest fix that keeps
        the direction and forgets the size?" Ask an LLM to derive cosine
        similarity from the plain dot product step by step, then have it
        argue whether transformers should have used cosine similarity
        instead of the scaled dot product for attention scores, and why
        they didn't.
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer shows the normalization step clearly, then explains
        that dropping magnitude also drops information the model can learn
        to use, which is the actual tradeoff behind the "cheapest fix"
        framing.
    - prompt: >-
        You met two scoring machines tonight: Bahdanau's learned alignment
        score and Vaswani's scaled dot product. Ask an LLM to "name the
        machine you just used" for each, explaining what Bahdanau's small
        feedforward scorer computes versus what the dot product computes,
        and why the field moved from one to the other.
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer treats both as similarity meters with different
        internals, one learned end to end as a tiny network, one a fixed
        geometric operation, and explains the dot product's win as speed
        and parallelism, not accuracy alone.
  papers:
    - title: Attention Is All You Need
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762
      hook: >-
        This is tonight's meter wearing a softmax. Read section 3.2.1 for
        the sqrt(d_k) scaling and you'll see the exact fix for the magnitude
        drift the beat "what the meter gets wrong" warned you about.
    - title: >-
        Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and
        Translate
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1409.0473
      hook: >-
        Before the dot product was the shortcut, alignment scores were
        computed by a small learned network comparing decoder state to
        encoder annotations. Read this to see what "the machine you just
        used" replaced, and why replacing it mattered.
    - title: Dot products and duality
      url: https://www.3blue1brown.com/lessons/dot-products
      hook: >-
        The projection-times-length picture and the sign behavior, aligned
        positive, perpendicular zero, opposed negative, is the exact visual
        grammar behind "turn the probe and feel it." Read it to make the
        sweep permanent.
  projects:
    - title: Build the meter and break it
      brief: >-
        In NumPy, build three scoring functions on a handful of toy word
        vectors: plain dot product, cosine similarity, and scaled dot
        product with sqrt(d_k). Rank the same query against your vector set
        under each meter, then rescale one candidate vector's magnitude
        without changing its direction and rerun all three rankings.
        Finish line: a table showing the dot product's ranking flip when
        magnitude changes while cosine similarity's ranking holds steady,
        proving what the cheapest fix actually buys you.
    - title: One head, by hand
      brief: >-
        Implement a single attention head from scratch in NumPy on a short
        toy sequence of 5 to 8 token vectors you make up yourself: compute
        query-key dot products, scale by sqrt(d_k), softmax, and use the
        weights to blend value vectors. Finish line: two attention-weight
        heatmaps for the same sequence, one with scaling and one without,
        showing the unscaled version collapsing toward one-hot picks as you
        raise the vector dimension.

input.content:
schemaVersion: 1
session: dotprod
lanes:
  llm:
    - prompt: >-
        Tonight's aha was that the dot product is a similarity meter: two
        vectors pointed the same way score high, perpendicular vectors score
        zero, opposed vectors score negative. Ask your LLM to walk through
        "the score behind the song" using scaled dot-product attention:
        why does the transformer use this exact meter to compare a query
        against every key, and why divide by sqrt(d_k) before the softmax?
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer names the meter's blind spot directly: raw dot products
        grow with vector length and dimension, so the sqrt(d_k) division is
        the fix that keeps the softmax from saturating, not a magic constant.
    - prompt: >-
        The breadcrumb question was: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the
        direction and forgets the size? Ask your LLM to derive cosine
        similarity from tonight's dot product formula, then give you one
        real example where two documents or vectors have a high dot product
        but a low cosine similarity, and explain what that mismatch reveals
        about "what the meter gets wrong."
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        The answer should show the division by both vector norms explicitly
        and produce a concrete pair of vectors where length inflates the raw
        dot product while cosine similarity reports the true alignment.
    - prompt: >-
        You turned the probe and felt the sign flip from positive to zero to
        negative as an angle swept past 90 degrees. Ask your LLM to name
        three other places in machine learning where this same meter shows
        up under a different name (not attention), and for each one, explain
        whether it uses raw dot products or a normalized version like cosine
        similarity, and why that choice was made.
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        Expect it to name things like word embedding similarity, recommender
        systems, or kernel methods, and to explicitly connect each choice
        back to whether magnitude carries real signal or is noise to discard.
  papers:
    - title: Attention Is All You Need
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762
      hook: >-
        This is where tonight's meter gets a job: scaled dot-product
        attention is exactly the score you just built, divided by sqrt(d_k)
        and fed into a softmax to weigh every key against a query.
    - title: >-
        Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and
        Translate
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1409.0473
      hook: >-
        Before the dot product got the job, a small learned network scored
        alignment between decoder and encoder states. Read this to see the
        meter you built tonight replace a more expensive machine, not invent
        the idea of scoring similarity.
    - title: Dot products and duality
      url: https://www.3blue1brown.com/lessons/dot-products
      hook: >-
        The geometric picture behind "turn the probe and feel it": projection
        times length, with the same sign behavior you swept through by hand.
        Worth a rewatch now that you have felt it instead of just seeing it.
  projects:
    - title: Cosine versus raw meter search
      brief: >-
        Take a small set of word or sentence vectors (GloVe, word2vec, or
        even hand-built bag-of-words counts work). For a query vector, rank
        every other vector twice: once by raw dot product, once by cosine
        similarity. Find at least one case where the two rankings disagree,
        and print the vector norms involved to show the length is what
        caused the flip. Finish line: a printed table of both rankings plus
        the norms that explain the disagreement.
    - title: Scaled dot-product attention from scratch
      brief: >-
        In plain NumPy, build a single attention head: random query, key,
        and value matrices, compute QK^T, divide by sqrt(d_k), softmax it,
        multiply by V. Then rerun it without the sqrt(d_k) division and plot
        the softmax output for both. Finish line: a heatmap or printed array
        showing the unscaled version producing a much peakier, closer to
        one-hot distribution than the scaled version, proving the fix from
        "the score behind the song" actually does something.

schemaVersion: 1
session: dotprod
lanes:
  llm:
    - prompt: >-
        Tonight's session called the raw dot product "the score behind the
        song": it multiplies aligned direction by both vectors' lengths, so a
        vector that is merely longer can outscore one that is merely more
        aligned. Ask an LLM to explain why that makes the raw score
        untrustworthy as a similarity meter, then ask it whether the
        sqrt(d_k) division in scaled dot-product attention is the same
        "cheapest fix that keeps the direction and forgets the size" as
        cosine similarity, or a different fix solving a different problem.
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer keeps the two fixes separate: cosine similarity divides
        by both vector lengths and genuinely forgets size, while sqrt(d_k)
        only tames the variance of the score as dimension grows and keeps
        size information alive. It should give a concrete numeric case where
        length alone flips a ranking.
    - prompt: >-
        Using tonight's "turn the probe and feel it" image, where rotating a
        vector makes the dot product rise, cross zero, and go negative, ask
        an LLM for two example vectors with a high dot product but low cosine
        similarity, and two more with a low dot product but high cosine
        similarity. Have it explain in both cases which part of the probe
        reading (direction or size) is doing the misleading work.
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        The reply gives actual numbers, computes both the dot product and
        cosine similarity for each pair, and states plainly that cosine is
        dot(a,b)/(|a||b|), the direction-only reading of the same probe.
    - prompt: >-
        Tell an LLM you just learned to "name the machine you just used":
        softmax(QK^T/sqrt(d_k))V, built entirely from the dot product as a
        similarity meter. Ask it to walk through the formula term by term and
        say exactly where the raw meter appears, where the size-fix lives,
        and where scores turn into weights that get "what the meter gets
        wrong" corrected before anything downstream trusts them.
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer names QK^T as the bank of dot-product similarity
        scores, sqrt(d_k) as the size-control step, and softmax as the step
        converting scores into a weighted average, in that order.
  papers:
    - title: Attention Is All You Need
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762
      hook: >-
        This is where tonight's meter goes to work: the QK^T/sqrt(d_k) term
        is the exact scaled dot-product score this session built by hand,
        now running as the core comparison inside a transformer.
    - title: >-
        Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and
        Translate
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1409.0473
      hook: >-
        Before anyone wrote a dot product into an alignment score, this
        paper scored decoder-encoder pairs with a learned function instead.
        Read it to see the machine you just named wasn't inevitable, it was
        a choice that got simplified into a dot product later.
    - title: Dot products and duality
      url: https://www.3blue1brown.com/lessons/dot-products
      hook: >-
        The projection-times-length picture here is the same sweep you just
        turned by hand tonight, with the sign behavior (aligned positive,
        perpendicular zero, opposed negative) laid out as one continuous
        geometric story instead of three separate facts to memorize.
  projects:
    - title: Build the meter and its cheap fix
      brief: >-
        In NumPy, hand-build five or six small vectors standing in for
        short documents. Compute the full dot-product similarity matrix,
        then the cosine similarity matrix, "the cheapest fix that keeps the
        direction and forgets the size." Find a pair where the two matrices
        rank neighbors differently, then rescale the longer vector to unit
        length and show the dot product ranking now agrees with cosine.
        Finish line: print both rankings side by side before and after the
        rescale, and the disagreement should vanish once size is removed.
    - title: Build the machine you just named
      brief: >-
        Implement scaled dot-product attention from scratch in NumPy on a
        toy Q, K, V (four tokens, eight dimensions each). Compute the
        softmax weights from the raw QK^T scores, then again from the
        scores divided by sqrt(d_k), at a larger dimension like 64. Finish
        line: print the entropy of each softmax weight distribution; the
        unscaled, high-dimension version should collapse toward a near-zero
        entropy one-hot pick while the scaled version stays smoother,
        showing the fix earning its place in the formula.

session: dotprod
lanes:
  llm:
    - prompt: >-
        We called the dot product a similarity meter and watched it turn
        into a score behind the song. Walk me through scaled dot-product
        attention in "Attention Is All You Need" line by line: which
        vectors are the probes, which are being measured, and where the
        divide by sqrt(d_k) shows up. Then answer this: if the meter's
        reading grows with vector length, why does that break the softmax
        it feeds, and how does the sqrt(d_k) division count as "the
        cheapest fix"?
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer names query and key vectors explicitly, shows the
        dot product as the raw meter reading before softmax, and explains
        the scaling as damage control on variance, not a cosmetic tweak.
    - prompt: >-
        Tonight's breadcrumb question was: what is the cheapest fix that
        keeps the direction and forgets the size? Explain cosine similarity
        to me as that fix applied to the similarity meter from our
        session, then give me a case where forgetting the size is wrong,
        a case where a longer vector should score higher, not just a more
        aligned one.
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer defines cosine similarity as dot product divided by
        the product of norms, then gives a concrete example (like
        confidence-weighted embeddings) where dropping magnitude throws
        away real signal.
    - prompt: >-
        We named the machine we used tonight: the dot product doing
        learned similarity scoring inside attention. Bahdanau et al.'s 2014
        alignment model scored decoder-encoder pairs before the dot-product
        version existed. Compare Bahdanau's additive scoring function to
        the dot-product score in Vaswani et al., and tell me what the
        field gained by switching machines, not just what changed.
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer identifies additive attention's extra learned
        weight matrix and nonlinearity, contrasts it with the dot
        product's cheapness, and names parallelizability or matrix-multiply
        efficiency as the real gain, not just novelty.
  papers:
    - title: Attention Is All You Need
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762
      hook: >-
        This is the score behind the song made explicit: read the
        attention section slowly and you will see tonight's meter, query
        dotted with key, doing the entire alignment job inside a
        transformer.
    - title: >-
        Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and
        Translate
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1409.0473
      hook: >-
        Before the dot product was the machine, this paper's alignment
        score was. Read it to see the same job, scoring how well two
        things match, solved with a different meter, so you feel what the
        dot product actually bought later.
    - title: Dot products and duality
      url: https://www.3blue1brown.com/lessons/dot-products
      hook: >-
        This is the sweep you just turned with your own hands, drawn out
        as projection times length. Watch it once more now that you know
        the sign flip is the meter reporting aligned, perpendicular, or
        opposed.
  projects:
    - title: Build the meter before and after the fix
      brief: >-
        Take a small set of toy vectors (5 to 10, some short, some long,
        at varying angles) and compute a full pairwise dot-product table
        with NumPy. Then compute the cosine-similarity table for the same
        vectors. Print both tables side by side and rank each vector's
        top-3 nearest neighbors under each meter. Finish line: find and
        report at least one pair whose ranking flips between the two
        tables, and explain in one sentence why size caused the flip.
    - title: Scale or collapse
      brief: >-
        Implement scaled dot-product attention from scratch in NumPy on a
        toy sequence of 4 to 6 vectors with dimension d_k you control.
        Run it once with the sqrt(d_k) division and once without, for
        d_k values of 8, 64, and 512. Print the softmax output each time.
        Finish line: show that the unscaled version's softmax collapses
        toward one-hot as d_k grows while the scaled version stays soft,
        proving the sqrt(d_k) fix earns its place.

      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762
      hook: >-
        This is tonight's meter wearing a softmax. Read section 3.2.1 for
        the sqrt(d_k) scaling and you'll see the exact fix for the magnitude
        drift the beat "what the meter gets wrong" warned you about.
    - title: >-
        Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and
        Translate
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1409.0473
      hook: >-
        Before the dot product was the shortcut, alignment scores were
        computed by a small learned network comparing decoder state to
        encoder annotations. Read this to see what "the machine you just
        used" replaced, and why replacing it mattered.
    - title: Dot products and duality
      url: https://www.3blue1brown.com/lessons/dot-products
      hook: >-
        The projection-times-length picture and the sign behavior, aligned
        positive, perpendicular zero, opposed negative, is the exact visual
        grammar behind "turn the probe and feel it." Read it to make the
        sweep permanent.
  projects:
    - title: Build the meter and break it
      brief: >-
        In NumPy, build three scoring functions on a handful of toy word
        vectors: plain dot product, cosine similarity, and scaled dot
        product with sqrt(d_k). Rank the same query against your vector set
        under each meter, then rescale one candidate vector's magnitude
        without changing its direction and rerun all three rankings.
        Finish line: a table showing the dot product's ranking flip when
        magnitude changes while cosine similarity's ranking holds steady,
        proving what the cheapest fix actually buys you.
    - title: One head, by hand
      brief: >-
        Implement a single attention head from scratch in NumPy on a short
        toy sequence of 5 to 8 token vectors you make up yourself: compute
        query-key dot products, scale by sqrt(d_k), softmax, and use the
        weights to blend value vectors. Finish line: two attention-weight
        heatmaps for the same sequence, one with scaling and one without,
        showing the unscaled version collapsing toward one-hot picks as you
        raise the vector dimension.

input.content:
schemaVersion: 1
session: dotprod
lanes:
  llm:
    - prompt: >-
        We called the dot product a similarity meter and watched it turn
        into a score behind the song. Walk me through scaled dot-product
        attention in "Attention Is All You Need" line by line: which
        vectors are the probes, which are being measured, and where the
        divide by sqrt(d_k) shows up. Then answer this: if the meter's
        reading grows with vector length, why does that break the softmax
        it feeds, and how does the sqrt(d_k) division count as "the
        cheapest fix"?
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer names query and key vectors explicitly, shows the
        dot product as the raw meter reading before softmax, and explains
        the scaling as damage control on variance, not a cosmetic tweak.
    - prompt: >-
        Tonight's breadcrumb question was: what is the cheapest fix that
        keeps the direction and forgets the size? Explain cosine
        similarity to me as that fix applied to the similarity meter from
        our session, then give me a case where forgetting the size is
        wrong, a case where a longer vector should score higher, not just
        a more aligned one.
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer defines cosine similarity as dot product divided by
        the product of norms, then gives a concrete example, like
        confidence-weighted embeddings, where dropping magnitude throws
        away real signal.
    - prompt: >-
        We named the machine we used tonight: the dot product doing
        learned similarity scoring inside attention. Bahdanau et al.'s
        2014 alignment model scored decoder-encoder pairs before the
        dot-product version existed. Compare Bahdanau's additive scoring
        function to the dot-product score in Vaswani et al., and tell me
        what the field gained by switching machines, not just what
        changed.
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer identifies additive attention's extra learned
        weight matrix and nonlinearity, contrasts it with the dot
        product's cheapness, and names parallelizability or matrix
        multiply efficiency as the real gain, not just novelty.
  papers:
    - title: Attention Is All You Need
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762
      hook: >-
        This is the score behind the song made explicit: read the
        attention section slowly and you will see tonight's meter, query
        dotted with key, doing the entire alignment job inside a
        transformer.
    - title: >-
        Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and
        Translate
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1409.0473
      hook: >-
        Before the dot product was the machine, this paper's alignment
        score was. Read it to see the same job, scoring how well two
        things match, solved with a different meter, so you feel what the
        dot product actually bought later.
    - title: Dot products and duality
      url: https://www.3blue1brown.com/lessons/dot-products
      hook: >-
        This is the sweep you just turned with your own hands, drawn out
        as projection times length. Watch it once more now that you know
        the sign flip is the meter reporting aligned, perpendicular, or
        opposed.
  projects:
    - title: Build the meter before and after the fix
      brief: >-
        Take a small set of toy vectors, 5 to 10, some short, some long,
        at varying angles, and compute a full pairwise dot-product table
        with NumPy. Then compute the cosine-similarity table for the same
        vectors. Print both tables side by side and rank each vector's
        top-3 nearest neighbors under each meter. Finish line: find and
        report at least one pair whose ranking flips between the two
        tables, and explain in one sentence why size caused the flip.
    - title: Scale or collapse
      brief: >-
        Implement scaled dot-product attention from scratch in NumPy on a
        toy sequence of 4 to 6 vectors with a dimension d_k you control.
        Run it once with the sqrt(d_k) division and once without, for
        d_k values of 8, 64, and 512. Print the softmax output each time.
        Finish line: show that the unscaled version's softmax collapses
        toward one-hot as d_k grows while the scaled version stays soft,
        proving the sqrt(d_k) fix earns its place.

input.content:
schemaVersion: 1
session: dotprod
lanes:
  llm:
    - prompt: >-
        Tonight's meter is the dot product: it reads out alignment times
        length, "the score behind the song." Ask the LLM to build you two
        vectors that point in the exact same direction but give wildly
        different dot-product readings, and to explain in the meter's own
        terms why the number moved even though the "song" didn't change.
        Then push it on the breadcrumb question: what is the cheapest fix
        that keeps the direction and forgets the size?
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer produces two same-direction, different-length
        vectors, shows the dot product scaling with length, and names
        cosine similarity (dividing by the norms) as the cheap fix, not a
        new machine.
    - prompt: >-
        In "Name the machine you just used" you learned that scaled
        dot-product attention is a bank of similarity meters run in
        parallel, one per query-key pair. Ask the LLM to walk through why
        Vaswani et al. divide by sqrt(d_k) before the softmax, using the
        "meter" and "sweep" vocabulary from tonight: what reading is the
        scaling correcting for, and is it the same problem as the
        breadcrumb question or a different one?
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A strong answer distinguishes the two fixes: sqrt(d_k) tames the
        variance of dot products as dimension grows, while
        normalizing/cosine similarity removes vector length entirely, and
        explains why attention keeps some size information instead of
        fully normalizing.
    - prompt: >-
        Recreate "Turn the probe and feel it" verbally: ask the LLM to
        narrate what happens to a dot-product reading as one vector is
        rotated through 360 degrees against a fixed probe, in the same
        sign language this session used, aligned positive, perpendicular
        zero, opposed negative, and then ask it where a reader would get
        fooled if they mistook a long vector for a "more similar" one.
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        Look for a clean sign trace across the sweep plus an explicit
        warning that a big dot product can mean "longer," not "more
        aligned," which is exactly the meter's blind spot named in the
        last beat.
  papers:
    - title: 3Blue1Brown, "Dot products and duality"
      url: https://www.3blue1brown.com/lessons/dot-products
      hook: >-
        This is the visual grammar your sweep echoed tonight, projection
        times length, watch it once as animation instead of a fixed frame
        and the sign flips in "Turn the probe and feel it" will click
        harder.
    - title: >-
        Bahdanau, Cho, Bengio, "Neural Machine Translation by Jointly
        Learning to Align and Translate"
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1409.0473
      hook: >-
        Before anyone wrote the score as a dot product, this paper shipped
        a learned alignment score doing the same job by hand, read it to
        see "the score behind the song" invented from scratch, one layer
        before it got simplified into the meter you used tonight.
    - title: Vaswani et al., "Attention Is All You Need"
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762
      hook: >-
        This is "the machine you just named," in full: query-key dot
        products divided by sqrt(d_k) feeding a softmax, read section 3.2.1
        to see your meter running at scale, then decide for yourself
        whether the scaling is the same fix as tonight's breadcrumb
        question or a cousin of it.
  projects:
    - title: Build the cheapest fix
      brief: >-
        In NumPy, make three word-ish vectors: two pointing the same
        direction but different lengths, one pointing a different
        direction. Compute raw dot products for all pairs, then compute
        cosine similarity for the same pairs. Finish line: a printed table
        showing the raw dot product ranking the same-direction pair with
        different lengths as "more similar" than it should be, while
        cosine similarity correctly reports the two same-direction vectors
        as near-identical regardless of length, the cheap fix caught on
        camera.
    - title: Scale the meter bank
      brief: >-
        Using NumPy, hand-build a tiny scaled dot-product attention over 4
        to 6 toy word vectors (random or one-hot-ish): compute raw QK^T,
        then QK^T / sqrt(d_k), run both through softmax, and compare the
        two attention weight matrices. Finish line: show that the
        unscaled version produces near one-hot, overconfident attention
        weights as d_k grows while the scaled version stays spread out,
        proof that "Name the machine you just used" needs its own fix,
        distinct from cosine similarity.

schemaVersion: 1
session: dotprod
lanes:
  llm:
    - prompt: >-
        We called the dot product a similarity meter and watched it turn
        into a score behind the song. Walk me through scaled dot-product
        attention in "Attention Is All You Need" line by line: which
        vectors are the probes, which are being measured, and where the
        divide by sqrt(d_k) shows up. Then answer this: if the meter's
        reading grows with vector length, why does that break the softmax
        it feeds, and how does the sqrt(d_k) division count as "the
        cheapest fix"?
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer names query and key vectors explicitly, shows the
        dot product as the raw meter reading before softmax, and explains
        the scaling as damage control on variance, not a cosmetic tweak.
    - prompt: >-
        Tonight's breadcrumb question was: what is the cheapest fix that
        keeps the direction and forgets the size? Explain cosine
        similarity to me as that fix applied to the similarity meter from
        our session, then give me a case where forgetting the size is
        wrong, a case where a longer vector should score higher, not just
        a more aligned one.
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer defines cosine similarity as dot product divided by
        the product of norms, then gives a concrete example, like
        confidence-weighted embeddings, where dropping magnitude throws
        away real signal.
    - prompt: >-
        We named the machine we used tonight: the dot product doing
        learned similarity scoring inside attention. Bahdanau et al.'s
        2014 alignment model scored decoder-encoder pairs before the
        dot-product version existed. Compare Bahdanau's additive scoring
        function to the dot-product score in Vaswani et al., and tell me
        what the field gained by switching machines, not just what
        changed.
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer identifies additive attention's extra learned
        weight matrix and nonlinearity, contrasts it with the dot
        product's cheapness, and names parallelizability or matrix
        multiply efficiency as the real gain, not just novelty.
  papers:
    - title: Attention Is All You Need
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762
      hook: >-
        This is the score behind the song made explicit: read the
        attention section slowly and you will see tonight's meter, query
        dotted with key, doing the entire alignment job inside a
        transformer.
    - title: >-
        Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and
        Translate
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1409.0473
      hook: >-
        Before the dot product was the machine, this paper's alignment
        score was. Read it to see the same job, scoring how well two
        things match, solved with a different meter, so you feel what the
        dot product actually bought later.
    - title: Dot products and duality
      url: https://www.3blue1brown.com/lessons/dot-products
      hook: >-
        This is the sweep you just turned with your own hands, drawn out
        as projection times length. Watch it once more now that you know
        the sign flip is the meter reporting aligned, perpendicular, or
        opposed.
  projects:
    - title: Build the meter before and after the fix
      brief: >-
        Take a small set of toy vectors, 5 to 10, some short, some long,
        at varying angles, and compute a full pairwise dot-product table
        with NumPy. Then compute the cosine-similarity table for the same
        vectors. Print both tables side by side and rank each vector's
        top-3 nearest neighbors under each meter. Finish line: find and
        report at least one pair whose ranking flips between the two
        tables, and explain in one sentence why size caused the flip.
    - title: Scale or collapse
      brief: >-
        Implement scaled dot-product attention from scratch in NumPy on a
        toy sequence of 4 to 6 vectors with a dimension d_k you control.
        Run it once with the sqrt(d_k) division and once without, for
        d_k values of 8, 64, and 512. Print the softmax output each time.
        Finish line: show that the unscaled version's softmax collapses
        toward one-hot as d_k grows while the scaled version stays soft,
        proving the sqrt(d_k) fix earns its place.

input.content:
schemaVersion: 1
session: dotprod
lanes:
  llm:
    - prompt: >-
        Tonight's meter is the dot product: it reads out alignment times
        length, "the score behind the song." Ask the LLM to build you two
        vectors that point in the exact same direction but give wildly
        different dot-product readings, and to explain in the meter's own
        terms why the number moved even though the "song" didn't change.
        Then push it on the breadcrumb question: what is the cheapest fix
        that keeps the direction and forgets the size?
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer produces two same-direction, different-length
        vectors, shows the dot product scaling with length, and names
        cosine similarity (dividing by the norms) as the cheap fix, not a
        new machine.
    - prompt: >-
        In "Name the machine you just used" you learned that scaled
        dot-product attention is a bank of similarity meters run in
        parallel, one per query-key pair. Ask the LLM to walk through why
        Vaswani et al. divide by sqrt(d_k) before the softmax, using the
        "meter" and "sweep" vocabulary from tonight: what reading is the
        scaling correcting for, and is it the same problem as the
        breadcrumb question or a different one?
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A strong answer distinguishes the two fixes: sqrt(d_k) tames the
        variance of dot products as dimension grows, while
        normalizing/cosine similarity removes vector length entirely, and
        explains why attention keeps some size information instead of
        fully normalizing.
    - prompt: >-
        Recreate "Turn the probe and feel it" verbally: ask the LLM to
        narrate what happens to a dot-product reading as one vector is
        rotated through 360 degrees against a fixed probe, in the same
        sign language this session used, aligned positive, perpendicular
        zero, opposed negative, and then ask it where a reader would get
        fooled if they mistook a long vector for a "more similar" one.
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        Look for a clean sign trace across the sweep plus an explicit
        warning that a big dot product can mean "longer," not "more
        aligned," which is exactly the meter's blind spot named in the
        last beat.
  papers:
    - title: 3Blue1Brown, "Dot products and duality"
      url: https://www.3blue1brown.com/lessons/dot-products
      hook: >-
        This is the visual grammar your sweep echoed tonight, projection
        times length, watch it once as animation instead of a fixed frame
        and the sign flips in "Turn the probe and feel it" will click
        harder.
    - title: >-
        Bahdanau, Cho, Bengio, "Neural Machine Translation by Jointly
        Learning to Align and Translate"
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1409.0473
      hook: >-
        Before anyone wrote the score as a dot product, this paper shipped
        a learned alignment score doing the same job by hand, read it to
        see "the score behind the song" invented from scratch, one layer
        before it got simplified into the meter you used tonight.
    - title: Vaswani et al., "Attention Is All You Need"
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762
      hook: >-
        This is "the machine you just named," in full: query-key dot
        products divided by sqrt(d_k) feeding a softmax, read section 3.2.1
        to see your meter running at scale, then decide for yourself
        whether the scaling is the same fix as tonight's breadcrumb
        question or a cousin of it.
  projects:
    - title: Build the cheapest fix
      brief: >-
        In NumPy, make three word-ish vectors: two pointing the same
        direction but different lengths, one pointing a different
        direction. Compute raw dot products for all pairs, then compute
        cosine similarity for the same pairs. Finish line: a printed table
        showing the raw dot product ranking the same-direction pair with
        different lengths as "more similar" than it should be, while
        cosine similarity correctly reports the two same-direction vectors
        as near-identical regardless of length, the cheap fix caught on
        camera.
    - title: Scale the meter bank
      brief: >-
        Using NumPy, hand-build a tiny scaled dot-product attention over 4
        to 6 toy word vectors (random or one-hot-ish): compute raw QK^T,
        then QK^T / sqrt(d_k), run both through softmax, and compare the
        two attention weight matrices. Finish line: show that the
        unscaled version produces near one-hot, overconfident attention
        weights as d_k grows while the scaled version stays spread out,
        proof that "Name the machine you just used" needs its own fix,
        distinct from cosine similarity.

        what the meter gets wrong: it rewards length, not just alignment.
        Ask your LLM to walk through a concrete pair of vectors where the
        badly-aligned but long vector beats the well-aligned but short one
        on raw dot product, then ask it to name the cheapest fix that keeps
        the direction and forgets the size.
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer produces an actual numeric example where the ranking
        flips, names cosine similarity as the fix, and can say in one
        sentence why dividing by the norms removes length from the score
        without touching direction.

        Tonight's beat "Name the machine you just used" pointed at scaled
        dot-product attention. Ask your LLM to show you, step by step, how
        a query vector and a set of key vectors turn into the same kind of
        similarity meter reading we built by hand, then how those readings
        become the softmax weights that pick out a value.
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-

        divide by sqrt(d_k) as protection against the meter's length
        problem at high dimension, and ends with the softmax turning scores
        into a weighted average, not a single winner.

input.content:
schemaVersion: 1
session: dotprod
lanes:
  llm:
    - prompt: >-
        You watched a dot product score three candidate arrows against a
        probe, and you saw the raw score change when a candidate's length
        changed even though its direction never moved. Explain that problem
        to the LLM in your own words, then propose your own fix for a
        scorer that should care about direction and not size. Have it
        stress-test your fix: what happens when a candidate is very short
        but dead-on, or very long but off by 40 degrees?
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer pushes back with a boundary case you did not think of,
        like a candidate of zero length, rather than just agreeing with your
        proposal.
    - prompt: >-
        Ask the LLM for a preview of next session's machine: you will see
        tonight's meter in its work uniform, a dot product, divided by
        sqrt(d_k), then softmaxed, scoring every word against every other
        word. Ask specifically why dividing by sqrt(d_k) matters, given
        what you now know about length inflating a raw score.
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer ties the sqrt(d_k) division back to length inflating
        scores in higher dimensions, not just a definition of attention.
    - prompt: >-
        Reread the session's "Same meter, different aisle" section, where
        the probe became a job description and the candidates became
        resumes. Ask the LLM to invent a third aisle for the same meter,
        one neither of you has used yet, and to walk through what plays the
        probe and what plays the candidates there.
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer names a concrete new domain, like matching photos to
        captions, and correctly maps which list is the probe and which are
        the candidates.
  papers:
    - title: Attention Is All You Need
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762
      hook: >-
        This is where tonight's meter shows up in its work uniform: scaled
        dot-product attention, scoring every word against every other word
        before dividing by sqrt(d_k) and passing through a softmax. Section
        3.2.1 has the exact formula tonight's sweep was rehearsing.
    - title: >-
        Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and
        Translate
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1409.0473
      hook: >-
        Before a dot product did the scoring, alignment scores here were
        learned by a small feedforward network instead. Worth a read for
        the version of tonight's aha that shipped first, and what the dot
        product later made cheaper.
    - title: Dot products and duality
      url: https://www.3blue1brown.com/lessons/dot-products
      hook: >-
        The sign behavior you swept through tonight, positive, zero,
        negative, has a full geometric account here: projection times
        length. Watch it if you want the picture underneath the arithmetic
        you just ran.
  projects:
    - title: Break the raw score, then fix it
      brief: >-
        Reuse the probe and candidate vectors from tonight's dot-alignment
        interactive. Write a short script that computes the raw dot product
        of each candidate against the probe, then stretches one candidate's
        length by 5x and prints the ranking flip you saw onscreen. Then
        implement the fix you proposed to the LLM and print the same
        ranking again. Finish line: a before-and-after table where
        stretching a vector's length changes the raw ranking but leaves
        your fixed ranking untouched.
    - title: Score every word against every other word
      brief: >-
        Build a tiny scaled dot-product attention pass by hand on five or
        six short word vectors (random numbers are fine). Compute every
        pairwise dot product, divide by sqrt(d_k), then softmax each row.
        Finish line: an attention matrix whose rows each sum to 1, with the
        largest weight in each row landing on the word pair you would
        expect to be most related.

schemaVersion: 1
session: dotprod
lanes:
  llm:
    - prompt: >-
        We called the dot product a similarity meter, and the breadcrumb
        question was: what is the cheapest fix that keeps the direction and
        forgets the size? I will tell you the fix I came up with; your job
        is to stress-test it. Show me a case where the raw dot product and
        my proposed fix would rank two candidate vectors in a different
        order, and check whether my fix really keeps only the direction.
        Use the aligned /
        perpendicular / opposed sign behavior from the sweep to check your
        example.
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer produces two vectors where a long-but-misaligned
        vector beats a short-but-aligned one on raw dot product, then
        flips the ranking once the learner's fix removes the length, and explains the flip using
        length times projection rather than just asserting it.
    - prompt: >-
        The meter shows up in transformers as scaled dot-product attention,
        next session's machine if you want a preview tonight: query dot
        key, divided by sqrt(d_k), fed into a softmax. Explain to me why the
        divide-by-sqrt(d_k) step exists, what breaks in the softmax if you
        skip it, and how that connects back to the meter reading you get
        from turning the probe in the geometric sweep.
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer talks about dot products growing with dimension so
        the pre-softmax scores get too large and saturate the softmax into
        near one-hot outputs, and ties that growth back to the
        length-times-projection picture rather than treating sqrt(d_k) as a
        magic constant.
    - prompt: >-
        I just named the machine I've been using all along: the dot
        product. Give me three places outside language models where the
        same meter shows up under a different name in the same aisle, like
        we did in "same meter, different aisle." For each one tell me what
        gets treated as query and what gets treated as key, and what a
        high score means in that domain.
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer names concrete domains (recommendation matching,
        document search, image embedding lookup, and similar) and for each
        one correctly identifies the two vectors being compared and states
        what a high score physically means there, not just "similarity."
  papers:
    - title: Attention Is All You Need
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762
      hook: >-
        Read the attention equation once and you will see tonight's meter
        in its work uniform: a dot product, divided by sqrt(d_k), then
        softmaxed, scoring every word against every other word.
    - title: >-
        Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and
        Translate
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1409.0473
      hook: >-
        Before the dot product got the job, alignment scoring was done a
        different way. Reading this next to tonight's session shows you
        that the similarity meter is the idea; the dot product is just the
        cheapest gauge anyone found for it.
    - title: Dot products and duality
      url: https://www.3blue1brown.com/lessons/dot-products
      hook: >-
        This is the geometric grammar underneath the sweep you did
        tonight: projection times length, with the same aligned-positive,
        perpendicular-zero, opposed-negative sign behavior, drawn out
        instead of described.
  projects:
    - title: Build the honest-look ranking flip
      brief: >-
        In NumPy, make five short "document" vectors and one "query"
        vector, all with different lengths on purpose. Rank the documents
        two ways: by raw dot product with the query, and by the cheapest
        fix you proposed for the breadcrumb. Print both rankings side by
        side. Finish line: at least one document changes rank between the
        two lists, and you can point to its length as the reason, proving
        your proposed fix really does forget the size and keep only the
        direction.
    - title: Turn the probe into a tiny attention layer
      brief: >-
        Using only NumPy, encode four short toy "words" as small random
        vectors, pick one as the query, and compute scaled dot-product
        attention (query dot each key, divide by sqrt(d_k), softmax) over
        the other three as keys and values. Finish line: print the
        attention weights and confirm the word whose vector you deliberately
        made most aligned with the query gets the highest weight, so you

input.content:
schemaVersion: 1
session: dotprod
lanes:
  llm:
    - prompt: >-
        Tonight's probe-and-candidates sweep showed that a longer candidate
        arrow can out-score a better-aligned one, even though the dot
        product is supposed to be measuring how well two things point the
        same way. Propose your own fix to the score that would stop length
        from winning arguments it shouldn't. Then ask the LLM to try to
        break your fix: can it describe a candidate that is short and only
        loosely aligned, but that your fixed score still ranks above a
        long, tightly aligned one?
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A strong exchange ends with you naming exactly what your fix keeps
        (direction) and what it throws away, plus at least one edge case
        where your first version of the fix still gets fooled by length.
    - prompt: >-
        In "Same meter, different aisle," the same dot product that scored
        a probe against songs got pointed at a resume and a job instead.
        Argue with an LLM about whether that is a genuine transfer of the
        same machine or just reusing the word "score": what has to be true
        about how a resume and a job get turned into lists of numbers
        before a dot product between them means anything, the way it meant
        something between the probe and the candidates tonight?
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer surfaces that the two lists need to be built on the
        same features, counted the same way, before the dot product can
        compare them at all, mirroring how the probe and candidates shared
        one space tonight.
  papers:
    - title: Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and Translate
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1409.0473
      hook: >-
        Before the dot product got the job, this paper's alignment score
        did the same work by hand, comparing a decoder state against every
        encoder state to decide what to weigh. It is the direct ancestor of
        tonight's probe-against-candidates sweep, worth reading to see the
        same comparison problem solved with a different, learned scoring
        function before the dot product's shortcut took over.
    - title: Dot products and duality
      url: https://www.3blue1brown.com/lessons/dot-products
      hook: >-
        The geometric picture behind tonight's honest look: projection
        times length, with the same sign flip you swept through tonight
        (aligned positive, perpendicular zero, opposed negative) drawn out
        visually instead of read off a moving probe. Good for re-anchoring
        tonight's aha in pictures before you build anything.
    - title: Attention Is All You Need
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762
      hook: >-
        A peek at next session: tonight's meter shows up here in its work
        uniform. You will see the same probe-times-candidate score, scaled
        by sqrt(d_k) (the square root of the key dimension, a size
        correction) and passed through softmax (the squash that turns
        scores into weights summing to one), scoring every word in a
        sentence against every other word.
  projects:
    - title: Break the sweep, then fix it
      brief: >-
        Rebuild tonight's probe-and-candidates sweep in NumPy: one probe
        vector, three candidate vectors, dot products as scores. Break it
        on purpose by making the worst-aligned candidate twice as long as
        the others and watch it win anyway. Then invent your own
        adjustment to the score so length stops deciding the winner and
        only direction does, and rerun the sweep. Finish line: a printed
        ranking that survives you doubling any single candidate's length.
    - title: Turn the job aisle into code
      brief: >-
        Build a tiny job-aisle matcher: represent one job posting and three
        candidate resumes as short feature vectors (skills as counts or
        0/1 flags), score each resume against the posting with a dot
        product the way tonight's probe scored candidates, and rank them.
        Finish line: a printed ranking plus one sentence explaining why the
        top-scoring resume is not automatically the best fit if it just
        lists more skills than the others.

input.content:
schemaVersion: 1
session: dotprod
lanes:
  llm:
    - prompt: >-
        Tonight's meter (the score behind the song, multiply the matching
        entries and add the products) has a cheat: stretch a candidate's
        arrow and its raw score climbs even if the direction is worse.
        Propose your own cheapest fix that would stop the stretch from
        working without throwing away the direction, then hand it to an LLM
        and have it try to break your fix with a specific pair of arrows.
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good answer produces a concrete pair of arrows, one stretched, and
        shows whether your fix's score still ranks them the same way as
        before, not just a restatement of why stretching worked.
    - prompt: >-
        Tonight's honest look showed that while every candidate held the
        same length, the score's sign, positive, zero, negative, told you
        everything about alignment between the probe and a song like Night
        Drive or Static. Argue with an LLM about whether "similarity meter"
        is a fair name for the raw multiply-and-add score once lengths are
        allowed to differ. See if it can talk you out of your position with
        a concrete counterexample pair of arrows.
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A good exchange ends with an actual pair of arrows, not just a
        concession, where the raw score's ranking would mislead someone
        trusting it as an honest meter.
    - prompt: >-
        In "Same meter, different aisle," the session asked you to fill in
        the mapping: "In the job aisle, which list plays the probe?" Pick
        your own second aisle, recipes, sports stats, anything with
        matching feature lists, answer that same fill-in for your aisle,
        and defend to an LLM that the multiply-and-add recipe from "the
        score behind the song" still applies unchanged. Make it try to find
        a feature list where the mapping breaks.
      whatGoodLooksLike: >-
        A solid answer names the exact probe list and candidate list in your
        new aisle and shows the multiply-and-add recipe producing a
        sensible score, not just a general claim that the analogy
        transfers.
  papers:
    - title: Dot products and duality
      url: https://www.3blue1brown.com/lessons/dot-products
      hook: >-
        Tonight's honest look gave you the sign story, aligned positive,
        perpendicular zero, opposed negative, but only while every arrow
        held the same length. This walkthrough gives you the geometric half
        underneath it, the dot product as one arrow's shadow length along
        another times that other arrow's own length, which is exactly the
        piece that let stretching cheat the meter.
    - title: >-
        Neural Machine Translation by Jointly Learning to Align and
        Translate
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1409.0473
      hook: >-
        Tonight's meter used one fixed recipe, multiply the matching
        entries and add the products, to score two lists against each
        other. This paper scores alignment a different way, with a small
        trained function standing in for the fixed formula. Read it to see
        the fork in the road the fixed multiply-and-add recipe eventually
        won.
    - title: Attention Is All You Need
      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762
      hook: >-
        A peek at next session: you will meet tonight's meter in its work
        uniform, scaled dot-product attention. It is the same
        multiply-and-add score, shrunk by dividing by the square root of
        the arrow length, sqrt(d_k), a fixed shrinking number, then run
        through softmax, a squash that turns raw scores into weights
        summing to one, so every word can score every other word at once.
  projects:
    - title: Rebuild the arena in code
      brief: >-
        Rebuild tonight's dot-alignment arena in NumPy. Give your amber
        probe (taste) a short list of numbers, and give Night Drive, Glass
        Rain, Slow Burn, and Static their own lists. Write the
        multiply-and-add score for the probe against all four, sweep the
        probe through a full rotation in fixed steps, and print the score
        at each angle. Confirm the honest look's sign story (positive when
        aligned, zero at ninety degrees, negative when opposed) holds while
        every candidate keeps the same length. Then stretch Night Drive's
        list alone and find the exact length where it overtakes whichever
        candidate led before. Finish line: a print or plot showing the
        ranking flip, labeled with the length that caused it.
    - title: Score a different aisle
      brief: >-
        Take the different aisle from tonight's session, resumes against a
        job posting, and build a tiny matching demo. Invent three or four
        short resumes and one job posting, each turned by hand into a small
        list of numbers (years of experience, count of a key skill word,
        whatever you pick). Score every resume against the job with the
        same multiply-and-add recipe from "the score behind the song," and
        rank the resumes by raw score. Finish line: a printed ranking, plus
        one sentence saying whether you would trust that ranking if one
        resume's numbers were simply bigger across the board.

      url: https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762

        uniform, scaled dot-product attention. It is the same
        multiply-and-add score, shrunk by dividing by the square root of

      brief: >-

        list of numbers (years of experience, count of a key skill word,
        whatever you pick). Score every resume against the job with the

        rank the resumes by raw score. Finish line: a printed ranking, plus
        one sentence saying whether you would trust that ranking if one
        resume's numbers were simply bigger across the board.
